name: Cyprus
tagline: An island at the crossroads of three continents, Greek and Turkish, divided by a line drawn in green.
summary: Cyprus has been shaped by everyone who has ever sailed the eastern Mediterranean. Settled by some of the oldest Neolithic communities outside the Near East, it became a string of Greek city-kingdoms famous for the worship of Aphrodite, then passed in turn to Assyrians, Egyptians, Persians, the Hellenistic Ptolemies, Rome and Byzantium, becoming one of the first lands governed by a Christian convert. In the Middle Ages it was a Crusader kingdom under the French Lusignan dynasty, then a Venetian colony, before falling to the Ottomans in 1571 and passing to British administration in 1878. Independence came in 1960 under a complex Greek-Turkish power-sharing constitution guaranteed by Britain, Greece and Turkey, but the settlement broke down within three years; intercommunal violence in 1963-64 brought the deployment of the UN peacekeeping force UNFICYP, and a Greek-junta-backed coup in 1974 was followed by a Turkish military intervention that partitioned the island. A Turkish Cypriot state proclaimed in 1983 is recognised only by Turkey and was declared legally invalid by the United Nations; the Republic of Cyprus joined the European Union in 2004 with the island still divided.

founding.label: Independence under the Zürich-London Agreements
founding.detail: The Republic of Cyprus was born on 16 August 1960, when British colonial rule ended on the basis of compromise agreements reached at Zürich and London in 1959. The new state had an intricate constitution sharing power between the Greek Cypriot majority and the Turkish Cypriot minority, a Treaty of Guarantee binding Britain, Greece and Turkey to uphold its independence and territorial integrity, and a provision allowing the United Kingdom to retain sovereignty over two military bases at Akrotiri and Dhekelia. The settlement explicitly ruled out both énosis (union with Greece), the goal of the Greek Cypriot nationalist movement, and partition. The arrangement proved fragile: within three years the power-sharing machinery had broken down, and the island has been entangled in one of the world's longest-running international disputes ever since.

quickFacts.label: Oldest settlement
quickFacts.value: Neolithic Choirokoitia, occupied from the 7th to the 4th millennium BCE

quickFacts.label: Greek city-kingdoms
quickFacts.value: Greek settlement from c. 1200 BCE; cult of Aphrodite centred on Paphos

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: Republic of Cyprus proclaimed 16 August 1960 under the Zürich-London Agreements

quickFacts.label: UN peacekeeping
quickFacts.value: UNFICYP established by Security Council Resolution 186, 4 March 1964

quickFacts.label: Division
quickFacts.value: Greek-junta coup and Turkish military intervention, July-August 1974

quickFacts.label: European Union
quickFacts.value: Joined the EU on 1 May 2004; EU law suspended in the north

eras.title: Neolithic Settlers, Greek City-Kingdoms and the Cult of Aphrodite
eras.standfirst: From one of the Mediterranean's oldest farming villages to a constellation of Greek city-kingdoms famed for the worship of the goddess of love.
body: Cyprus was settled remarkably early. At Choirokoitia, on the island's south coast, a Neolithic community of round stone houses flourished from roughly the seventh to the fourth millennium BCE, one of the most important prehistoric sites in the eastern Mediterranean and a window onto the spread of farming across the region. The island's copper, which may have given Cyprus its very name, drew it into the trade networks of the Bronze Age Near East, and its harbours linked the civilisations of the Aegean, the Levant and Egypt.

From around 1200 BCE, in the upheaval that followed the collapse of the Mycenaean palaces, Greek-speaking settlers arrived in numbers, and Greek became the dominant language of the island. They founded or refounded a network of city-kingdoms, among them Salamis, Kition, Paphos, Curium, Soli, Marion and Lapithos, each with its own king. Old Paphos became the great centre of the cult of Aphrodite, the goddess whom Greek myth held to have risen from the sea-foam off the Cypriot coast; her sanctuary, with origins traced to the twelfth century BCE, made the island a place of pilgrimage for the ancient Greek world.

Strategically placed and rich in copper, Cyprus rarely controlled its own destiny for long. Its kingdoms paid tribute to the Assyrians in the eighth and seventh centuries BCE, fell under Egyptian and then Persian overlordship, and shared in the Greek world's struggle against the Persian Empire. After the conquests of Alexander the Great the island passed to the Hellenistic kingdom of the Ptolemies of Egypt, who abolished the old city-kingdoms and ruled Cyprus as a province until Rome annexed it in 58 BCE.

events.title: The Neolithic settlement of Choirokoitia
events.summary: A farming community of round stone dwellings flourished at Choirokoitia, one of the best-preserved and most important prehistoric sites in the eastern Mediterranean, illuminating the early evolution of human society in the region.

events.title: Greek settlement and the city-kingdoms
events.summary: After the collapse of Mycenaean Greece, Greek-speaking settlers established the city-kingdoms of Cyprus, among them Salamis, Kition and Paphos, making Greek the island's predominant language.

events.title: The sanctuary of Aphrodite at Paphos
events.summary: Old Paphos became the principal centre of the worship of Aphrodite, whose temple bore witness to one of the oldest Mycenaean settlements and drew pilgrims from across the Greek world.

events.title: Assyrian, Egyptian and Persian overlordship
events.summary: The Cypriot kingdoms paid tribute successively to the Assyrian, Egyptian and Persian empires, sharing in the wider Greek world's long struggle against Persian domination.

events.title: Ptolemaic rule
events.summary: Following Alexander the Great's conquests, Cyprus passed to the Hellenistic Ptolemies of Egypt, who abolished the independent city-kingdoms and governed the island as a province until Rome took it in 58 BCE.

figures.name: Aphrodite (Kypris)
figures.role: Goddess of love, patron of the island
figures.blurb: Greek myth held that Aphrodite rose from the sea-foam off the coast of Cyprus, and her sanctuary at Old Paphos became one of the most renowned cult centres of the ancient Mediterranean. So closely was she tied to the island that she was known simply as Kypris, 'the Cyprian'.

figures.name: Evagoras I of Salamis
figures.blurb: The most celebrated of the Cypriot Greek kings, Evagoras made Salamis a centre of Hellenic culture and led resistance to Persian control of the island, becoming a symbol of Cyprus's Greek identity in the classical age.

pullquote.text: Since the start of the first millennium BCE the Greek language has been predominant in Cyprus, carried by settlers who founded its city-kingdoms.
pullquote.attribution: On the Greek settlement of Cyprus

eras.title: Roman and Byzantine Cyprus: An Early Christian Island
eras.standfirst: Under Rome and Byzantium, Cyprus became one of the first lands governed by a Christian convert and a stronghold of Greek Orthodox faith and art.
body: Rome annexed Cyprus in 58 BCE and, after a brief interlude, organised it as a senatorial province governed by a proconsul. The island prospered under Roman rule, and Nea Paphos, with its lavish floor mosaics, served as the Roman capital. According to the New Testament, when the apostles Paul and Barnabas, the latter a native Cypriot, travelled across the island around 45-47 CE they reached the proconsul Sergius Paulus at Paphos and won him to the new faith, making Cyprus an early scene of Christian conversion at the highest level of provincial government.

When the Roman world divided, Cyprus fell to the eastern, Byzantine empire, ruled from Constantinople. The Church of Cyprus secured a prized independence: it was recognised as autocephalous, governing itself rather than answering to a neighbouring patriarch, a status its archbishops have guarded ever since. Christianity reshaped the island's landscape, and in later centuries the painted churches of the Troödos mountains, decorated with some of the richest Byzantine and post-Byzantine murals to survive anywhere, would make the region one of the largest concentrations of Byzantine ecclesiastical art in existence.

Cyprus's wealth and position made it a target. From the seventh century Arab fleets raided the island repeatedly, and for some three centuries it was effectively a contested frontier zone between Byzantium and the Caliphate before Byzantine authority was firmly restored in 965. The empire's hold lasted until 1191, when a rebellious local governor's defiance gave a passing Crusader king the pretext to seize the island and change its course entirely.

events.title: Rome annexes Cyprus
events.summary: Cyprus was annexed by Rome and organised as a province; under Roman rule the island prospered, with Nea Paphos as its capital and a centre of the celebrated mosaic art later inscribed by UNESCO.

events.title: Paul and Barnabas convert the proconsul
events.summary: On their journey across Cyprus the apostles Paul and Barnabas reached the Roman proconsul Sergius Paulus at Paphos, an early instance of a senior Roman official embracing Christianity.

events.title: An autocephalous Church
events.summary: The Church of Cyprus was recognised as self-governing (autocephalous), free from the authority of any neighbouring patriarch, a distinction its archbishops have defended throughout the island's history.

events.title: Arab raids and a contested frontier
events.summary: From the mid-seventh century Cyprus suffered repeated Arab naval raids and for some three centuries lay as a contested zone between Byzantium and the Caliphate before Byzantine rule was firmly restored in 965.

events.title: The painted churches of the Troödos
events.summary: The mountain region of Troödos accumulated one of the largest groups of Byzantine churches and monasteries, richly decorated with murals later inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List.

figures.name: Saint Barnabas
figures.role: Apostle and patron of the Church of Cyprus
figures.blurb: A native of Cyprus and companion of Paul, Barnabas evangelised his homeland and is venerated as the founder of the Cypriot Church. The later discovery of his tomb near Salamis was used to support the Church of Cyprus's claim to autocephaly.

figures.name: Sergius Paulus
figures.blurb: Roman governor of Cyprus who, according to the Acts of the Apostles, was converted to Christianity by Paul at Paphos, an episode marking the island as an early outpost of the faith within the Roman administration.

pullquote.text: The Troödos region contains one of the largest groups of churches and monasteries of the former Byzantine Empire, richly decorated with murals.

eras.title: Crusader Kingdom, Venetian Colony and Ottoman Conquest
eras.standfirst: Seized by a Crusader king, ruled for three centuries by a French dynasty, sold to Venice and then taken by the Ottomans, Cyprus became a layered Mediterranean society.
body: In 1191, on his way to the Third Crusade, King Richard I of England seized Cyprus from its rebellious Byzantine governor. He soon sold the island, which passed in 1192 to Guy de Lusignan, the dispossessed French king of Jerusalem. The Lusignan dynasty ruled Cyprus as a Latin, feudal kingdom for nearly three centuries, building Gothic cathedrals at Nicosia and Famagusta and presiding over a wealthy crossroads of Mediterranean trade, while the Greek Orthodox majority lived under a French-speaking Catholic aristocracy.

As Lusignan power waned, the merchant republic of Venice extended its grip. In 1489 the Venetian-born widow of the last Lusignan king, Caterina Cornaro, ceded Cyprus to the Republic of Venice, which ruled the island as a fortified colony, prizing it as a forward base and a source of trade against the rising power of the Ottoman Empire. The Venetians ringed Nicosia and Famagusta with massive defensive walls that still stand, but invested little in the welfare of the Greek population.

The Ottoman Empire ended Venetian rule by force. An Ottoman army invaded in 1570, took Nicosia, and after a long and brutal siege captured Famagusta in 1571, completing the conquest. Ottoman rule lasted just over three centuries. The Latin Church was suppressed and the Orthodox Church restored to prominence, its archbishop recognised as leader (millet head) of the Greek Cypriots, while Turkish-speaking Muslims settled on the island, laying the demographic foundations of the two communities whose relationship would dominate modern Cypriot history.

events.title: Richard the Lionheart seizes Cyprus
events.summary: En route to the Third Crusade, King Richard I of England captured Cyprus from its Byzantine governor, ending Byzantine rule and opening the island to Latin Crusader control.

events.title: The Lusignan Kingdom of Cyprus
events.summary: Guy de Lusignan, former king of Jerusalem, took possession of Cyprus, founding a French feudal dynasty that ruled the island as a Latin Crusader kingdom for nearly three centuries.

events.title: Cyprus ceded to Venice
events.summary: Caterina Cornaro, widow of the last Lusignan king, ceded Cyprus to the Republic of Venice, which ruled it as a fortified colonial outpost and bulwark against the Ottomans.

events.title: Ottoman conquest of Cyprus
events.summary: An Ottoman army invaded in 1570, taking Nicosia, and after a long siege captured Famagusta in 1571, ending Venetian rule and beginning more than three centuries of Ottoman administration.

events.title: Two communities take shape
events.summary: Under Ottoman rule the Orthodox Church was restored and its archbishop made leader of the Greek Cypriots, while Muslim settlement established the Turkish Cypriot community alongside it.

figures.name: Guy de Lusignan
figures.blurb: A French knight and former king of Jerusalem, Guy acquired Cyprus in 1192 and founded the Lusignan dynasty, which transformed the island into a Latin feudal kingdom oriented toward Western Europe.

figures.name: Caterina Cornaro
figures.blurb: Venetian-born queen and widow of the last Lusignan king, Caterina ceded Cyprus to the Republic of Venice in 1489, ending the Crusader kingdom and bringing the island under Venetian rule.

pullquote.text: Cyprus passed from Crusader kingdom to Venetian colony to Ottoman province, each ruler leaving its mark on a layered Mediterranean society.
pullquote.attribution: On medieval and early modern Cyprus

eras.title: British Rule, the Demand for Énosis and the EOKA Campaign
eras.standfirst: Britain took over the island from the Ottomans in 1878 and held it as a colony, while many Greek Cypriots pressed for union with Greece and turned to armed struggle.
body: Britain assumed the administration of Cyprus in 1878 under the Cyprus Convention with the Ottoman Empire, which retained nominal sovereignty in exchange for Britain's promise of support against Russia. The island gave Britain a strategic station near the Suez route to India. When the Ottoman Empire entered the First World War against Britain in 1914, Britain annexed Cyprus outright; Turkey formally renounced any claim under the Treaty of Lausanne in 1923, and in 1925 Cyprus was declared a Crown Colony.

From early in the British period many in the Greek Cypriot majority, encouraged by the Orthodox Church, campaigned for énosis, the union of Cyprus with Greece. Demands intensified after the Second World War, and in 1950 the Church organised a plebiscite in which an overwhelming majority of Greek Cypriots voted for union. Britain, valuing the island as a military base, refused; the Turkish Cypriot minority, fearing absorption into a Greek state, opposed énosis and increasingly favoured partition (taksim).

In 1955 the frustration of the énosis movement turned to violence. EOKA, the National Organisation of Cypriot Fighters, led by the former Greek army officer Colonel Georgios Grivas and supported by Archbishop Makarios III, launched an armed campaign against British rule on 1 April 1955. The British exiled Makarios to the Seychelles in 1956 and met the insurgency with emergency measures, while intercommunal tension between Greek and Turkish Cypriots deepened. By 1958 Makarios had signalled he would accept independence in place of énosis, opening the way to a negotiated settlement.

events.title: Britain takes over Cyprus
events.summary: Under the Cyprus Convention, Britain assumed the administration of Cyprus from the Ottoman Empire, which retained nominal sovereignty, gaining a strategic Mediterranean base near the route to India.

events.title: British annexation
events.summary: When the Ottoman Empire joined the First World War against Britain, Britain annexed Cyprus; Turkey renounced its claim to the island in the 1923 Treaty of Lausanne.

events.title: Cyprus made a Crown Colony
events.summary: Cyprus was formally declared a British Crown Colony, consolidating colonial administration over the island.

events.title: The énosis plebiscite
events.summary: The Orthodox Church organised a plebiscite in which the overwhelming majority of Greek Cypriots voted for union with Greece; Britain refused, and Turkish Cypriots opposed it, sharpening communal division.

events.title: EOKA launches its armed campaign
events.summary: EOKA, led by Colonel Georgios Grivas with the support of Archbishop Makarios III, began an armed struggle against British rule in pursuit of énosis, opening four years of insurgency and repression.

figures.name: Archbishop Makarios III
figures.role: Ethnarch and first President of Cyprus
figures.blurb: Head of the Church of Cyprus and political leader of the Greek Cypriots, Makarios championed énosis, was exiled by the British, then accepted independence and became the republic's first president in 1960. He survived the 1974 coup against him and led Cyprus until his death.

figures.name: Georgios Grivas
figures.blurb: A Cypriot-born officer of the Greek army, Grivas organised and commanded the EOKA guerrilla campaign against British rule from 1955, remaining a hardline advocate of union with Greece long after independence.

pullquote.text: EOKA was an underground movement of Greek Cypriots dedicated to ending British rule and to achieving the eventual union of Cyprus with Greece.

eras.title: Independence, the Breakdown of Power-Sharing and UNFICYP
eras.standfirst: A guaranteed republic of two communities was created in 1960, but power-sharing collapsed within three years, bringing intercommunal violence, a Green Line and UN peacekeepers.
body: The road to independence ran through Switzerland and Britain. In February 1959 Greece and Turkey reached a compromise at Zürich, endorsed days later by the British government and Cypriot leaders in London. The resulting settlement created an independent Republic of Cyprus on 16 August 1960, with a constitution dividing offices between the Greek Cypriot majority and Turkish Cypriot minority and reserving the presidency for a Greek Cypriot and the vice-presidency for a Turkish Cypriot, each with a veto. A Treaty of Guarantee bound Britain, Greece and Turkey to uphold the new state, which undertook not to join in union with any other country or to be partitioned; Britain retained two Sovereign Base Areas at Akrotiri and Dhekelia.

The constitution proved unworkable. In late 1963 President Makarios proposed amendments to ease the deadlocks built into the power-sharing rules; Turkish Cypriots saw the changes as a threat to their guaranteed rights, and in December 1963 intercommunal violence erupted, beginning in Nicosia and spreading across the island. A ceasefire line through the capital, drawn on a map in green, gave the lasting term 'Green Line'. Turkish Cypriots withdrew from the organs of government and many moved into defended enclaves, leaving the bicommunal republic effectively broken.

With fighting threatening to draw in Greece and Turkey, the matter went to the United Nations. On 4 March 1964 the Security Council unanimously adopted Resolution 186, recommending, with the consent of the Government of Cyprus, the creation of a United Nations Peace-keeping Force in Cyprus (UNFICYP) and the appointment of a mediator. UNFICYP became operational on 27 March 1964 with the task of using its best efforts to prevent a recurrence of fighting and to help restore law and order; it has remained on the island ever since, the dispute outlasting decades of negotiation.

events.title: The Zürich-London Agreements
events.summary: Greece and Turkey reached a compromise at Zürich, endorsed in London by Britain and Cypriot leaders, setting the terms for an independent Cyprus that excluded both énosis and partition.

events.title: Independence and the Treaty of Guarantee
events.summary: The Republic of Cyprus became independent under a Greek-Turkish power-sharing constitution; the Treaty of Guarantee bound Britain, Greece and Turkey to its independence and territorial integrity, and Britain kept two Sovereign Base Areas.

events.title: Breakdown and the Green Line
events.summary: After President Makarios proposed constitutional changes, intercommunal violence broke out in December 1963; Turkish Cypriots withdrew from government and a ceasefire line dubbed the 'Green Line' was drawn through Nicosia.

events.title: UN Security Council Resolution 186 establishes UNFICYP
events.summary: The Security Council unanimously recommended, with the consent of the Government of Cyprus, the creation of the UN Peace-keeping Force in Cyprus to prevent a recurrence of fighting and help restore order; UNFICYP became operational on 27 March 1964.

events.title: A UN mediator for the Cyprus question
events.summary: Resolution 186 also recommended that the Secretary-General designate a mediator to promote a peaceful and agreed settlement, beginning the long international effort to resolve the dispute.

figures.name: Fazıl Küçük
figures.blurb: Leader of the Turkish Cypriot community and the republic's first vice-president under the 1960 constitution, Küçük defended the community's guaranteed rights and opposed the constitutional changes that preceded the 1963 breakdown.

figures.name: Archbishop Makarios III
figures.role: President of the Republic of Cyprus
figures.blurb: As first president, Makarios led the new republic through its early crises, proposing the 1963 constitutional amendments that Turkish Cypriots rejected and presiding over the island as power-sharing collapsed and UN peacekeepers arrived.

pullquote.text: UNFICYP was set up by the Security Council in 1964 to prevent further fighting between the Greek Cypriot and Turkish Cypriot communities.

eras.title: The 1974 Division, the TRNC and the European Union
eras.standfirst: A coup and a military intervention partitioned the island; a breakaway state recognised only by Turkey was declared invalid by the UN, and a divided Cyprus joined the European Union.
body: On 15 July 1974 the military junta then ruling Greece, acting through the Cypriot National Guard, mounted a coup against President Makarios, aiming to bring about énosis; Makarios escaped and the coup installed a hardline nationalist. Citing its rights as a guarantor power under the 1960 Treaty of Guarantee, Turkey launched a military intervention on 20 July 1974, and in a second offensive in August its forces took control of the northern part of the island, roughly the upper third. The Greek junta fell within days, but the fighting left Cyprus partitioned along a ceasefire line and uprooted both communities: Greek Cypriots fled or were expelled from the north and Turkish Cypriots moved to it, a mass displacement that hardened the island's division.

After 1974 UNFICYP's role shifted to supervising the de facto ceasefire lines and maintaining the buffer zone that runs across the island, alongside humanitarian work. The political deadlock hardened. On 15 November 1983 the Turkish Cypriot leadership unilaterally proclaimed the 'Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus'. On 18 November 1983 the UN Security Council adopted Resolution 541, which considered that declaration legally invalid, called for its withdrawal, and called upon all states not to recognise any Cypriot state other than the Republic of Cyprus. To this day the entity is recognised only by Turkey.

Efforts at reunification continued under UN auspices. In 2004 the United Nations put forward a comprehensive settlement, the Annan Plan, to refound Cyprus as a bizonal, bicommunal federation. In simultaneous referendums on 24 April 2004 the plan was approved by Turkish Cypriots but rejected by about three-quarters of Greek Cypriots voting. A week later, on 1 May 2004, the Republic of Cyprus joined the European Union; the whole island is EU territory, but the application of EU law is suspended in the northern areas not under the effective control of the government. Cyprus remains divided, its reunification still the subject of international negotiation.

events.title: The Greek-junta-backed coup
events.summary: The military junta ruling Greece, through the Cypriot National Guard, overthrew President Makarios in pursuit of énosis; Makarios escaped abroad and a hardline nationalist was installed.

events.title: Turkish military intervention
events.summary: Invoking its guarantor rights under the 1960 Treaty of Guarantee, Turkey landed forces on 20 July 1974 and in a second August offensive took the northern third of the island, partitioning Cyprus and displacing both communities; UNFICYP was thereafter tasked with supervising the ceasefire lines and the buffer zone.

events.title: Declaration of the 'TRNC' and UN Resolution 541
events.summary: The Turkish Cypriot leadership proclaimed the 'Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus' on 15 November 1983; on 18 November the Security Council, in Resolution 541, considered the declaration legally invalid and called on all states not to recognise any Cypriot state other than the Republic of Cyprus.

events.title: The Annan Plan referendums
events.summary: A UN reunification plan was put to simultaneous referendums; Turkish Cypriots approved it but about three-quarters of Greek Cypriots voting rejected it, leaving the island divided on the eve of EU membership.

events.title: Accession to the European Union
events.summary: The Republic of Cyprus joined the EU; the whole island is EU territory, but the application of EU law is suspended in the northern areas not under the government's effective control.

figures.name: Rauf Denktaş
figures.blurb: The dominant Turkish Cypriot political figure of the late twentieth century, Denktaş proclaimed the 'Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus' in 1983 and led it for two decades, championing a separate Turkish Cypriot statehood that won recognition only from Turkey.

figures.name: Archbishop Makarios III
figures.blurb: Target of the 1974 coup that sought to unite Cyprus with Greece, Makarios escaped the island and returned after the junta's fall to resume the presidency of a now-partitioned republic, which he led until his death in 1977.

pullquote.text: The Security Council considered the 'Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus' legally invalid, and called upon all States not to recognize any Cypriot State other than the Republic of Cyprus.

figures.name: Aphrodite (Kypris)
figures.blurb: The Greek goddess of love whom myth held to have risen from the sea off Cyprus; her great sanctuary at Old Paphos made the island one of the most famous cult centres of the ancient Mediterranean.

figures.name: Archbishop Makarios III
figures.role: Ethnarch and first President of Cyprus
figures.blurb: Religious and political leader who championed énosis under British rule, accepted independence in 1960 to become the republic's first president, and survived the 1974 coup that preceded the island's partition.

figures.name: Georgios Grivas
figures.blurb: Cypriot-born Greek army officer who led the EOKA guerrilla campaign against British rule from 1955, an uncompromising advocate of union with Greece.

figures.name: Rauf Denktaş
figures.blurb: The foremost Turkish Cypriot politician of his era, who proclaimed the 'Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus' in 1983, a state recognised only by Turkey and declared legally invalid by the United Nations.
